questions.question: A Nash equilibrium is a strategy profile in which:
options: the combined payoff of all players is maximized
every player earns the same payoff
no player can improve their payoff by unilaterally changing their own strategy
every player uses a random strategy

explanations.A: Maximizing the joint payoff describes a cooperative/Pareto outcome, which often differs from the Nash equilibrium.
explanations.B: Equilibrium says nothing about equal payoffs; players can earn very different amounts at a Nash equilibrium.
explanations.C: Correct. Equilibrium means each player's strategy is a best response to the others, so no one can gain by deviating alone.
explanations.D: Equilibria can be pure or mixed; randomization is not required for a profile to be a Nash equilibrium.

questions.question: Two market makers each pick a quote width. The current profile is NOT a Nash equilibrium if:
options: at least one maker could raise their own profit by changing only their own quote
both happen to earn positive profit
the two quotes are different from each other
one maker earns more than the other

explanations.A: Correct. A profitable unilateral deviation by any player means the profile fails the no-deviation test, so it is not a Nash equilibrium.
explanations.B: Positive profit for both is fully compatible with equilibrium; profitability is not the test.
explanations.C: Different quotes can absolutely be an equilibrium; identical strategies are not required.
explanations.D: Unequal payoffs are common at equilibria and do not disqualify a profile.

questions.question: Player A's 'best response' to player B's strategy is the action that:
options: is chosen at random to stay unpredictable
maximizes player A's own payoff given B's strategy

explanations.A: Hurting B is only worthwhile if it helps A; a best response maximizes A's own payoff, not necessarily B's loss.
explanations.B: Randomizing is sometimes optimal, but the best response is defined by payoff maximization, not by unpredictability for its own sake.
explanations.C: Copying B is only a best response if it happens to maximize A's payoff; in general it does not.
explanations.D: Correct. A best response is the action that gives A the highest payoff, taking B's strategy as fixed.

questions.question: A strategy profile is a Nash equilibrium exactly when:
options: each player's strategy is a best response to the others' strategies
it gives the first mover an advantage

explanations.A: Pareto efficiency is a separate property; many Nash equilibria (e.g. the prisoner's dilemma) are not Pareto efficient.
explanations.B: Correct. Mutual best response is the defining condition: no player can do better given what everyone else plays.
explanations.C: Equilibria can involve mixed strategies, so 'pure only' is not the criterion.
explanations.D: First-mover advantage is a feature of some sequential games, not the definition of equilibrium.

questions.question: A strategy is strictly dominant for a player when it:
options: guarantees the player wins the game
is best only when opponents cooperate
gives a strictly higher payoff than any other strategy, no matter what opponents do

explanations.A: A dominant strategy maximizes your payoff but need not guarantee 'winning' — payoffs can still be low.
explanations.B: Being best only under cooperation is conditional superiority, not dominance.
explanations.C: Dominance is about payoffs across all opponent actions, not about meeting expectations.
explanations.D: Correct. Strict dominance means the strategy beats every alternative against every possible opponent choice.

questions.question: A strategy is dominated when:
options: it is chosen by most players
it is the riskiest available option
another strategy does at least as well against every opponent action and strictly better against some

explanations.A: Popularity is irrelevant to dominance; many players could be playing a poor strategy.
explanations.B: Riskiness alone does not make a strategy dominated; the comparison is about payoffs versus another strategy.
explanations.C: Correct. A dominated strategy is weakly beaten everywhere and strictly beaten somewhere by an alternative, so a rational player avoids it.
explanations.D: Domination is unrelated to whether the strategy is pure or mixed.

questions.question: No matter how a rival prices, lowering your spread always nets you more flow and more profit than keeping it wide. Lowering the spread is:

explanations.A: Correct. Since it beats the alternative regardless of the rival's price, lowering the spread is strictly dominant.
explanations.B: A mixed strategy randomizes; this is a single pure action that is always best.
explanations.C: A dominated strategy is one you'd never play; here lowering the spread is the one you'd always play.
explanations.D: It strictly raises your profit in every case, so it is highly relevant.

questions.question: Iterated elimination of strictly dominated strategies works because:
options: dominated strategies always yield zero payoff
a rational player never plays a strictly dominated strategy, and players know this about each other
it requires players to randomize uniformly

explanations.A: Dominated strategies can yield any payoff; they are simply beaten by another strategy.
explanations.B: Correct. Rational players drop strictly dominated strategies; common knowledge of rationality lets you remove them in rounds, sometimes pinning down the equilibrium.
explanations.C: The procedure need not lead to the cooperative outcome — it predicts rational play, which can be Pareto-inferior.
explanations.D: It is about removing bad strategies, not about uniform randomization.

questions.question: If both players in a two-player game have a strictly dominant strategy, then:
options: the profile of dominant strategies is the unique Nash equilibrium
the equilibrium must be in mixed strategies

explanations.A: Correct. Each player's dominant strategy is a best response to anything, so the combination is the unique Nash equilibrium.
explanations.B: An equilibrium does exist — each playing their dominant strategy is stable.
explanations.C: Dominant strategies can exist in non-zero-sum games; the prisoner's dilemma is not zero-sum.
explanations.D: Dominant strategies are pure, so the equilibrium is in pure strategies.

questions.question: A game is zero-sum when:
options: the players' payoffs always sum to the same constant (zero), so one's gain is the other's loss

explanations.A: Negative payoffs are not required; what matters is that gains and losses offset.
explanations.B: The sum is fixed by structure; randomness in payoffs is a different matter.
explanations.C: Mutual winning would make the sum positive, contradicting zero-sum.
explanations.D: Correct. Zero-sum (constant-sum) means total payoff is fixed, so any gain to one player is an exactly equal loss to the other.

questions.question: Which situation is best modeled as (approximately) zero-sum?
options: two firms investing jointly in a new market that grows the pie
two traders taking opposite sides of the same futures contract, ignoring fees
neighbors sharing the cost of a fence that benefits both
coworkers splitting a bonus pool that grows with total output

explanations.A: Joint investment that grows the market is positive-sum: both can gain.
explanations.B: Correct. One side's profit on the contract is the other side's loss, so ignoring fees the payoffs sum to zero.
explanations.C: Cost-sharing for mutual benefit creates joint surplus, so it is not zero-sum.
explanations.D: A bonus pool that grows with output is positive-sum, not a pure transfer.

questions.question: In a strictly zero-sum two-player game, if Row's expected payoff is +0.4, then Column's expected payoff is:

explanations.A: Zero would only follow if Row's payoff were also zero.
explanations.B: Both being +0.4 would sum to +0.8, violating the zero-sum condition.
explanations.C: Correct. Payoffs sum to zero, so Column's expected payoff is exactly the negative of Row's: -0.4.
explanations.D: It is fully determined: Column = -Row = -0.4.

questions.question: Why can two desks in a zero-sum game never both profit on average against each other?
options: because zero-sum games have no equilibrium
because total payoff is fixed at zero, so positive expected payoff for one forces negative for the other
because both must use dominant strategies

explanations.A: Randomization is allowed in zero-sum games; that is not the reason.
explanations.B: Zero-sum games do have equilibria (von Neumann's minimax theorem guarantees a value).
explanations.C: Correct. With a fixed total of zero, the expected payoffs must be negatives of each other, so they cannot both be positive.
explanations.D: Many zero-sum games have no dominant strategies; that is unrelated to the impossibility of mutual profit.

questions.question: The minimax theorem (von Neumann) guarantees that every finite two-player zero-sum game has:
options: a value achievable with (possibly mixed) optimal strategies for both players
a dominant strategy for each player

explanations.A: Correct. Every finite zero-sum game has a value, and each player has an optimal (possibly mixed) strategy securing it.
explanations.B: The theorem guarantees an equilibrium exists, not that none does.
explanations.C: Equilibria may require mixing (e.g. matching pennies has no pure equilibrium), so uniqueness in pure strategies is not guaranteed.
explanations.D: Dominant strategies need not exist; the guarantee is about the game's value, not dominance.

questions.question: In the one-shot prisoner's dilemma, the unique Nash equilibrium is:

explanations.A: Mutual cooperation is jointly better but not stable — each player gains by defecting unilaterally.
explanations.B: An asymmetric profile is unstable because the cooperator would switch to defect.
explanations.C: Alternating requires repetition and coordination; in the one-shot game it is not an equilibrium.
explanations.D: Correct. Defect strictly dominates cooperate for each player, so mutual defection is the unique Nash equilibrium.

questions.question: What makes the prisoner's dilemma a 'dilemma'?
options: the dominant-strategy equilibrium is worse for both players than an available cooperative outcome

explanations.A: There is an equilibrium (mutual defection); the dilemma is about its quality, not its absence.
explanations.B: Correct. Individually rational defection leads to an outcome both players dislike compared to mutual cooperation — that tension is the dilemma.
explanations.C: It is not zero-sum: mutual cooperation makes both better off, so total payoff varies.
explanations.D: Each player does have a dominant strategy (defect); that's precisely what drives the bad outcome.

questions.question: Two traders set quotes; payoffs are: both cooperate (4,4), both defect (2,2), defect alone (6,1). What is the Nash equilibrium?

explanations.A: Cooperate is not stable: against a cooperator, defecting earns 6 instead of 4.
explanations.B: An equilibrium does exist — mutual defection.
explanations.C: (6,1) is not an equilibrium because the player earning 1 would deviate to defect for 2.
explanations.D: Correct. Defect dominates (6>4 vs cooperate, 2>1 vs defect), so mutual defection (2,2) is the unique Nash equilibrium.

questions.question: In an indefinitely repeated prisoner's dilemma, cooperation can be sustained as an equilibrium when:
options: defection becomes a dominated strategy in each stage
the game is played exactly once
players are sufficiently patient and use strategies that punish defection

explanations.A: Defection is still dominant in each isolated stage; cooperation is sustained by future incentives, not by changing stage dominance.
explanations.B: A single play removes any future to leverage, so cooperation cannot be enforced.
explanations.C: Correct. With enough patience, the threat of future punishment (e.g. tit-for-tat or grim trigger) makes cooperation a best response.
explanations.D: The prisoner's dilemma is not zero-sum; mutual cooperation benefits both.

questions.question: A mixed strategy is:
options: a probability distribution over a player's available actions
always playing the same single action

explanations.A: Correct. A mixed strategy assigns probabilities to actions and the player randomizes accordingly.
explanations.B: Committing to one action is a pure strategy, the opposite of mixing.
explanations.C: Copying the last move is a reactive rule, not a probability distribution over your own actions.
explanations.D: Mixed strategies are chosen precisely to optimize expected payoff, so they do not ignore payoffs.

questions.question: In matching pennies, each player's unique-equilibrium probability of playing heads is:

explanations.A: 1/3 leaves a predictable bias an opponent can exploit; it is not the equilibrium.
explanations.B: Correct. Equal 50/50 mixing makes the opponent indifferent between heads and tails, the only unexploitable profile.
explanations.C: 2/3 is also exploitable and not symmetric with the equilibrium condition.
explanations.D: Probability 0 (never heads) is a pure, fully predictable strategy that is easily exploited.

questions.question: Why might a market maker deliberately randomize whether to quote tight or wide?
options: so a strategic counterparty cannot predict and exploit a fixed pattern
to guarantee a higher payoff every single round
because randomization is required by exchange rules

explanations.A: Correct. Unpredictability prevents an adversary from best-responding to a known pattern, which is the whole point of mixing.
explanations.B: Randomizing does not raise payoff every round; it protects expected payoff against exploitation.
explanations.C: No exchange rule mandates randomization; it is a strategic choice.
explanations.D: Mixing does not change whether the underlying game is zero-sum.

questions.question: A pure strategy differs from a mixed strategy in that a pure strategy:
options: is only valid in zero-sum games
specifies a single action with certainty

explanations.A: Pure strategies appear in all kinds of games, not only zero-sum ones.
explanations.B: Pure strategies can be optimal and winning; they don't always lose.
explanations.C: Randomization is the hallmark of mixed strategies, not pure ones.
explanations.D: Correct. A pure strategy commits to one action deterministically, with no randomization.

questions.question: In a mixed-strategy equilibrium, a player who randomizes over two actions must find that the two actions give:
options: equal expected payoff against the opponent's strategy
the maximum possible payoff for one of them only
different payoffs that change each round

explanations.A: Equal expected payoffs need not be zero.
explanations.B: Correct. A player only mixes if both actions yield equal expected payoff; otherwise they'd shift all weight to the better one.
explanations.C: If one action were strictly best, the player would play it purely instead of mixing.
explanations.D: At equilibrium the expected payoffs are equal and stable, not fluctuating.

questions.question: To solve for a player's equilibrium mixing probabilities, you typically set:
options: the variance of payoffs to one
the opponent's expected payoffs from each of their actions equal to each other

explanations.A: Setting your own payoffs to zero is not how mixing probabilities are derived.
explanations.B: Variance is not the quantity the indifference condition controls.
explanations.C: Correct. You choose your mix so the opponent is indifferent — their actions yield equal expected payoff — which pins down your probabilities.
explanations.D: 1/2 is only the answer in symmetric games; in general the equilibrium mix is solved, not assumed.

questions.question: Column's expected payoff from Left is 4p and from Right is 1 + 2p, where p is Row's probability of playing Up. The value of p that makes Column indifferent is:

explanations.A: At p = 1, Left gives 4 and Right gives 3, so Column strictly prefers Left; not indifferent.
explanations.B: At p = 0, Left gives 0 and Right gives 1, so Column strictly prefers Right; not indifferent.
explanations.C: Correct. Setting 4p = 1 + 2p gives 2p = 1, so p = 1/2 (both actions then pay 2).
explanations.D: At p = 1/4, Left gives 1 and Right gives 1.5, so Column still prefers Right; not indifferent.

questions.question: The standard method for solving a finite sequential game of perfect information is:
options: backward induction from the final decision nodes
ignoring all but the first move
flipping a coin at each node
maximizing the opponent's payoff at each node

explanations.A: Correct. You solve the last subgames first and fold optimal choices back toward the root — that is backward induction.
explanations.B: Ignoring later moves discards exactly the information that determines optimal earlier play.
explanations.C: Random play throws away the structure that perfect information provides.
explanations.D: Rational players maximize their own payoff, not the opponent's.

questions.question: A subgame perfect equilibrium is a refinement of Nash equilibrium that rules out:
options: non-credible threats — actions a player would not actually carry out

explanations.A: Mixed strategies are still allowed in subgame perfect equilibria.
explanations.B: First-mover advantage is a feature of some games, not something the refinement removes.
explanations.C: It refines Nash equilibria; every subgame perfect equilibrium is itself a Nash equilibrium.
explanations.D: Correct. Subgame perfection requires optimal play in every subgame, eliminating threats that would be irrational to execute.

questions.question: In a sequential game, Player 1 chooses In or Out. If In, Player 2 chooses Fight (-1,-1) or Accommodate (2,1); Out gives (0,3). By backward induction, Player 1:
options: chooses Out, because Player 2 will Fight
chooses In, because Player 2 will Accommodate for payoff 1 > -1
is indifferent between In and Out

explanations.A: Fighting gives Player 2 only -1 versus 1 from accommodating, so a 'Fight' threat is not credible; Out is not forced.
explanations.B: Correct. Player 2 prefers Accommodate (1 > -1), so Player 1 anticipates payoff 2 from In versus 0 from Out and chooses In.
explanations.C: Player 1 strictly prefers In (2) to Out (0), so there is no indifference.
explanations.D: The game has perfect information and a clear backward-induction solution; no randomization is needed.

questions.question: A threat is 'credible' in a sequential game when:
options: it is made before the game starts
it would hurt the opponent the most
carrying it out is actually in the threatener's own interest when the moment arrives

explanations.A: Volume or emphasis does not make a threat credible; incentives do.
explanations.B: Timing of the announcement does not establish credibility; the on-path incentive does.
explanations.C: Maximizing the opponent's harm is irrelevant if executing the threat hurts you more.
explanations.D: Correct. A credible threat is one the player would rationally execute when called upon, so it survives subgame perfection.

questions.question: In the sequential Stackelberg quantity game, the leader typically gains an advantage by:
options: committing to a larger quantity that forces the follower to produce less
waiting for the follower to move first

explanations.A: Producing nothing surrenders the market and the advantage entirely.
explanations.B: Matching the follower forfeits the commitment advantage of moving first.
explanations.C: Correct. By committing first to a high quantity, the leader shifts the follower's best response downward and captures more profit.
explanations.D: Waiting turns the leader into a follower, giving up the first-mover edge.

questions.question: In a first-price sealed-bid auction, the winner is the highest bidder and pays:

explanations.A: Correct. In a first-price auction the winner pays exactly the amount they bid.
explanations.B: Paying the second-highest bid is the second-price (Vickrey) rule, not first-price.
explanations.C: Winners pay their own bid, not an average across bidders.
explanations.D: The winner does pay; the auction is not free.

questions.question: In a second-price (Vickrey) sealed-bid auction, bidding your true value is:

explanations.A: Truthful bidding is optimal here; it is not a losing strategy.
explanations.B: Correct. Because you pay the second-highest bid, your own bid only affects whether you win, never the price — so truthful bidding can never hurt and is weakly dominant.
explanations.C: Its optimality does not depend on others underbidding; it holds against any opponent bids.
explanations.D: Truthful bidding is precisely the rational, dominant choice in a Vickrey auction.

questions.question: In a first-price auction with independent private values, a rational bidder should:
options: shade their bid below their value
bid above their value to ensure winning

explanations.A: Correct. Since you pay your bid, shading below value preserves positive expected surplus, trading some win probability for profit.
explanations.B: Bidding your value leaves zero surplus when you win, so it is not optimal.
explanations.C: Bidding above value risks paying more than the item is worth — a loss whenever you win.
explanations.D: Never bidding forfeits all expected surplus; the optimal bid is positive but shaded.

questions.question: With n symmetric bidders whose values are uniform on [0,1], the symmetric first-price equilibrium bid is b(v) = v·(n-1)/n. As the number of bidders n grows large, optimal shading:
options: stays constant at half of value
shrinks, so bids approach the true value

explanations.A: The factor (n-1)/n rises toward 1, so bids rise toward value, not toward zero.
explanations.B: Equilibrium bids stay below value; they never exceed it.
explanations.C: Half of value corresponds only to n = 2; the fraction changes with n.
explanations.D: Correct. (n-1)/n → 1 as n grows, so heavier competition forces bidders to shade less and bid closer to their true value.

questions.question: The revenue equivalence theorem says that, under standard assumptions (risk-neutral bidders, independent private values), first-price and second-price auctions yield:
options: always higher revenue in the first-price format
the same expected revenue to the seller
always higher revenue in the second-price format

explanations.A: Shading in first-price exactly offsets the higher nominal payments, so it is not systematically higher.
explanations.B: Correct. Under those assumptions both formats produce the same expected seller revenue, even though bidding behavior differs.
explanations.C: Second-price collects the second-highest value in expectation, matching — not beating — first-price.
explanations.D: Both formats generate positive expected revenue when bidders value the item.

questions.question: The winner's curse arises in common-value auctions because:
options: the seller always sets too high a reserve
the winner is the bidder who most overestimated the common value
values are perfectly known to all

explanations.A: No such ban exists; the curse occurs precisely because naive bidders can bid near their (inflated) estimates.
explanations.B: The curse comes from estimation bias among bidders, not from the seller's reserve.
explanations.C: Correct. When all bidders estimate a shared value, the winner tends to be the one with the highest (often too optimistic) estimate, so winning is bad news about the value.
explanations.D: If values were perfectly known there would be no estimation error and no curse.

questions.question: A rational bidder in a common-value auction protects against the winner's curse by:
options: bidding their raw estimate of value
bidding as if their estimate is the highest of all bidders, i.e. shading down further

explanations.A: Bidding the raw estimate ignores that winning means your estimate was likely too high.
explanations.B: Bidding above the estimate worsens the curse.
explanations.C: Correct. You should condition on the event of winning — that your estimate was the most optimistic — and shade your bid down accordingly.
explanations.D: More competitors makes winning even more selective for over-optimism, so the count matters and should increase caution.

questions.question: You can pay $3 to play a game that pays $10 with probability 0.2 and $0 otherwise. The expected net value of playing is:

explanations.A: Correct. Expected payout is 0.2×$10 = $2; subtracting the $3 cost gives a net of -$1, so the game is unfavorable.
explanations.B: +$2 would be the gross expected payout (0.2×10) minus only part of the cost; it ignores the full $3 stake.
explanations.C: +$7 misreads the payout as nearly certain; the win is only 20% likely.
explanations.D: A break-even of $0 would require an expected payout of $3, but it is only $2.

questions.question: A strategy A yields +$5 with probability 0.5 and -$3 with probability 0.5. Strategy B yields a sure +$1. A risk-neutral player should pick:
options: strategy A, with expected value +$1... tie, but A is chosen by coin flip
strategy A, because its expected value is +$1 which is higher than B
strategy B, because it has lower variance
strategy B, because its expected value of +$1 equals A's, so a risk-neutral player is indifferent

explanations.A: A's expected value is 0.5×5 + 0.5×(-3) = +$1, which ties B; there is no strict reason to pick A.
explanations.B: A's expected value is +$1, equal to B's, not higher.
explanations.C: Lower variance matters to a risk-averse player, but a risk-neutral player cares only about expected value, which is tied.
explanations.D: Correct. A's expected value is 0.5×5 + 0.5×(-3) = +$1, exactly equal to B's sure +$1, so a risk-neutral player is indifferent.

questions.question: In matching pennies, Row wins on a match (HH or TT) and Column wins on a mismatch (HT or TH). This game has:

explanations.A: Neither player has a dominant strategy; the best action depends entirely on the opponent's choice.
explanations.B: Correct. Against any pure profile one player always wants to switch, so there is no pure-strategy equilibrium — only the 50/50 mixed one.
explanations.C: There are zero pure equilibria, not two.
explanations.D: Interests are strictly opposed (zero-sum), so there is no cooperative equilibrium.

questions.question: In a pure coordination game where both players prefer to pick the same option (and either matched outcome is equally good), the Nash equilibria are:
options: the profiles where they choose differently
the two profiles where both choose the same option

explanations.A: There is also a mixed equilibrium, but the salient pure equilibria are the matched profiles; 'only mixed' is wrong.
explanations.B: Equilibria clearly exist: the two coordinated outcomes.
explanations.C: Mismatched profiles are not equilibria — either player would switch to coordinate.
explanations.D: Correct. Both-choose-A and both-choose-B are Nash equilibria, since given the other's choice each player wants to match.

questions.question: In the stag hunt, hunting stag together yields the highest payoff but requires both to cooperate, while hunting hare is safe alone. The game has:
options: two pure Nash equilibria: both stag (payoff-dominant) and both hare (risk-dominant)

explanations.A: Hare is not dominant; if you trust the other to hunt stag, stag is the better response.
explanations.B: Multiple equilibria exist, so 'none' is wrong.
explanations.C: Correct. Both-stag and both-hare are each Nash equilibria; stag is payoff-dominant while hare is the safer, risk-dominant choice.
explanations.D: The stag hunt is a cooperation/coordination game, not zero-sum.

questions.question: The Battle of the Sexes is a coordination game with conflicting preferences. It has:
options: two pure-strategy equilibria plus one mixed equilibrium
a single dominant strategy for each player

explanations.A: Correct. There are two pure equilibria (each player's preferred coordinated outcome) and a mixed equilibrium as well.
explanations.B: Two pure equilibria exist — the players still prefer coordinating to mismatching.
explanations.C: Neither player has a dominant strategy; the best action depends on the partner's choice.
explanations.D: Both players gain from coordinating, so the game is not zero-sum.

questions.question: An outcome is Pareto efficient when:
options: no player can be made better off without making another worse off
it is the unique Nash equilibrium

explanations.A: Equality of payoffs is unrelated to Pareto efficiency.
explanations.B: Correct. Pareto efficiency means there is no alternative that improves someone's payoff without harming someone else.
explanations.C: Zero total payoff describes zero-sum games, not Pareto efficiency.
explanations.D: Nash equilibria can be Pareto inefficient (prisoner's dilemma), so the two concepts are distinct.

questions.question: In a game with multiple Nash equilibria, which statement is true?
options: different equilibria can give different payoffs, and players may face a coordination problem over which one occurs
all equilibria must give identical payoffs
only one of them is actually a Nash equilibrium

explanations.A: Correct. Multiple equilibria can differ in payoffs, creating a selection/coordination problem about which one the players land on.
explanations.B: Equilibria can yield very different payoffs (e.g. stag hunt's stag vs hare).
explanations.C: All of them are genuine Nash equilibria by definition; multiplicity is allowed.
explanations.D: Having multiple equilibria does not change whether payoffs are zero-sum.

questions.question: Which is the clearest sign that a player should NOT play a particular pure strategy?
options: it has the highest possible payoff in one cell
the opponent also has that strategy available
another available strategy strictly dominates it

explanations.A: Being mixed is not a reason to avoid a strategy; mixing can be optimal.
explanations.B: A single high-payoff cell does not justify a strategy if it does poorly against other opponent actions.
explanations.C: What strategies the opponent has does not by itself make yours unplayable.
explanations.D: Correct. If another strategy strictly dominates it, the dominated strategy is never a best response and should not be played.

questions.question: Which is NOT a property of a two-player zero-sum game?
options: the players' payoffs sum to a constant
both players can simultaneously improve their payoffs through cooperation
one player's gain is the other's loss
an optimal value exists by the minimax theorem

explanations.A: Constant-sum payoffs is exactly the definition of zero-sum, so this IS a property.
explanations.B: Correct. In zero-sum games there is no joint gain from cooperation — improving one player necessarily worsens the other, so this is NOT a property.
explanations.C: Pure transfer of payoff is a defining property of zero-sum games.
explanations.D: The minimax theorem guarantees a value for finite zero-sum games, so this IS a property.

questions.question: If a player's two actions give expected payoffs of 5 and 3 against the opponent's current strategy, a best-responding player should:

explanations.A: Mixing is only optimal when the actions tie in expected payoff; here they do not.
explanations.B: Playing the worse action (3) is strictly suboptimal.
explanations.C: Correct. With unequal expected payoffs the best response is the pure action with the higher value, 5.
explanations.D: Alternating would put weight on the inferior action, lowering expected payoff.

questions.question: In rock-paper-scissors, the symmetric Nash equilibrium has each player choosing each action with probability:

explanations.A: 1/2 cannot cover three actions summing to 1.
explanations.B: 1/4 each sums to 3/4, not a valid distribution over three actions.
explanations.C: Correct. Equal 1/3-1/3-1/3 mixing makes the opponent indifferent across rock, paper, and scissors, the only unexploitable profile.
explanations.D: At the symmetric equilibrium the mix is fixed at 1/3 each, independent of the opponent.

questions.question: Two rival HFT firms decide whether to over-invest in speed (an arms race). Each does better cutting the other off regardless of the rival's choice, but mutual over-investment wastes money for both. This is structurally:

explanations.A: Correct. Over-investing is individually dominant yet mutual over-investment is worse for both than mutual restraint — the prisoner's dilemma structure.
explanations.B: Coordination games reward matching choices; here each has a dominant move that leads to a jointly bad outcome.
explanations.C: It is not zero-sum: mutual restraint would make both better off, so total payoff varies.
explanations.D: There is an equilibrium — mutual over-investment.

questions.question: A penalty taker can shoot Left or Right; the goalie can dive Left or Right. The taker scores if they pick the opposite side from the goalie's dive. In equilibrium, both randomize. This is structurally a:

explanations.A: The players' interests are opposed, not aligned, so it is not a coordination game.
explanations.B: No action dominates; that is precisely why the equilibrium is mixed.
explanations.C: There is no dominant 'defect' action; the best choice depends on the opponent, unlike a prisoner's dilemma.
explanations.D: Correct. The taker wants to mismatch and the goalie wants to match — exactly the matching-pennies/zero-sum structure with a mixed equilibrium.

questions.question: In a 2x2 zero-sum game with no pure equilibrium, the equilibrium mixing probabilities are found by:
options: setting all payoffs equal to zero
making each player indifferent between their own actions via the opponent's mix
choosing the action with the single highest payoff

explanations.A: Payoffs are given by the game; you do not set them to zero.
explanations.B: Correct. Each player picks a mix that makes the other indifferent between their actions, which jointly determines the equilibrium probabilities.
explanations.C: Picking the single highest-payoff cell ignores the opponent's response and is not how mixed equilibria are solved.
explanations.D: 50/50 is the answer only in symmetric games; in general the mix must be solved for.

questions.question: Row's payoff to Up is 2q and to Down is 3 - q (q = Column's probability of Left). Row is made indifferent at q =

explanations.A: At q = 1/2, Up gives 1 and Down gives 2.5, so Row strictly prefers Down; not indifferent.
explanations.B: At q = 0, Up gives 0 and Down gives 3, so Row strictly prefers Down.
explanations.C: At q = 3/4, Up gives 1.5 and Down gives 2.25, so Row still prefers Down.
explanations.D: Correct. Setting 2q = 3 - q gives 3q = 3, so q = 1 (both actions then pay 2).

questions.question: A best-response function maps:
options: a player's payoff to a probability
the opponents' strategies to the player's optimal strategy

explanations.A: It outputs an optimal strategy, not a single probability from a payoff.
explanations.B: Best responses depend on opponents' strategies, not on the clock.
explanations.C: Correct. The best-response correspondence takes the opponents' strategies as input and returns the player's payoff-maximizing strategy.
explanations.D: It is a deliberate optimization, not a mapping from noise.

questions.question: Nash's existence theorem guarantees that every finite game (finitely many players and actions) has:
options: at least one Nash equilibrium, possibly in mixed strategies
a dominant strategy for every player
no equilibrium unless it is zero-sum

explanations.A: Correct. Nash proved every finite game has at least one equilibrium, allowing mixed strategies.
explanations.B: Many finite games have multiple equilibria or none in pure strategies; uniqueness and purity are not guaranteed.
explanations.C: Dominant strategies often do not exist; the theorem does not promise them.
explanations.D: The guarantee covers all finite games, not just zero-sum ones.

questions.question: A coin pays $4 on heads and costs you $2 on tails, with a fair coin. The expected value per flip is:

explanations.A: +$2 ignores the loss on tails.
explanations.C: +$3 double-counts the gain and omits the tail loss.
explanations.D: $0 would require the gain and loss to cancel, but +4 and -2 do not.

questions.question: A market maker captures a $0.02 edge on 70% of trades and loses $0.05 (adverse selection) on the other 30%. Expected edge per trade is:

explanations.B: +$0.014 is only the favorable term; it omits the 30% loss.
explanations.C: -$0.015 is only the adverse term; it omits the 70% gain.
explanations.D: +$0.02 is the gross edge on a good trade, not the probability-weighted average.

questions.question: You face a choice: a guaranteed $40, or a 50% chance at $100 (else $0). A risk-neutral decision maker should:
options: take the gamble, since its expected value $50 exceeds $40

explanations.A: A risk-neutral player maximizes expected value, and $40 is below the gamble's $50.
explanations.B: Both options have positive value; refusing forgoes gains.
explanations.C: The expected values differ ($50 vs $40), so there is no indifference.
explanations.D: Correct. The gamble's EV is 0.5×100 = $50, which beats the sure $40 for a risk-neutral player.

questions.question: A risk-averse player might prefer a guaranteed $40 over a 50/50 gamble worth $50 in expectation because:
options: they value the reduction in uncertainty more than the extra expected $10
the gamble is actually worth less than $40 in expectation

explanations.A: Risk aversion is not a math error; the player can correctly see the $50 EV and still decline.
explanations.B: Correct. A risk-averse player's utility is concave, so the certainty premium can outweigh the higher expected money value.
explanations.C: The gamble's expected value is genuinely $50; preference, not arithmetic, drives the choice.
explanations.D: Risk aversion is a coherent, common preference, not irrationality.

questions.question: Weak dominance differs from strict dominance in that a weakly dominant strategy:
options: is strictly better in every case
is at least as good in every case and strictly better in at least one

explanations.A: A dominant strategy is never worse; this describes a dominated strategy.
explanations.B: Strictly-better-everywhere is the definition of strict dominance, not weak dominance.
explanations.C: Correct. Weak dominance means never worse and sometimes strictly better, whereas strict dominance is strictly better everywhere.
explanations.D: Dominance describes pure-action comparisons and does not require randomization.

questions.question: Why can the ability to publicly commit to an action be valuable in a strategic game?
options: it never helps; flexibility is always better
commitment can change opponents' best responses in your favor, even though it removes your own options
it forces the game to be simultaneous

explanations.A: Flexibility is not always better; strategically tying your hands can improve outcomes (Schelling's insight).
explanations.B: Commitment does not make a game zero-sum.
explanations.C: Correct. A credible commitment alters what opponents expect and how they respond, which can raise your payoff despite reducing your own flexibility.
explanations.D: Commitment typically introduces sequential structure, not simultaneity.

questions.question: In repeated prisoner's dilemma tournaments, tit-for-tat performs well because it is:
options: nice (never defects first), retaliatory, forgiving, and clear
always cooperating regardless of the opponent

explanations.A: Correct. Tit-for-tat starts cooperatively, punishes defection immediately, forgives once cooperation resumes, and is simple to read — a robust combination.
explanations.B: Always defecting forgoes the gains from sustained mutual cooperation.
explanations.C: Random play cannot establish the stable cooperation that makes tit-for-tat strong.
explanations.D: Unconditional cooperation is exploitable by defectors; tit-for-tat retaliates.

questions.question: A poker player who only ever bets with strong hands is exploitable because opponents can:
options: force the player to randomize by rule
fold whenever the player bets, denying value and never paying off bluffs

explanations.A: No rule forces randomization; the player should mix voluntarily to avoid exploitation.
explanations.B: Opponents can and should adapt to the predictable pattern.
explanations.C: Always calling a value-only bettor loses money; the correct exploit is to fold.
explanations.D: Correct. A perfectly predictable 'bet = strong' pattern lets opponents fold to bets, so the player wins less and can never bluff — mixing in some bluffs restores balance.

questions.question: In game theory optimal (GTO) poker, balancing your betting range with bluffs and value bets is meant to:
options: make opponents indifferent between calling and folding, so they cannot exploit you
eliminate the need to ever fold

explanations.A: No strategy wins every hand; GTO secures an unexploitable expected outcome, not certainty.
explanations.B: Correct. Proper bluff-to-value ratios make the opponent indifferent, removing any exploitable best response.
explanations.C: Balancing is about unexploitability, not maximizing variance.
explanations.D: Folding remains part of optimal play; balance does not eliminate it.

questions.question: Trading between two counterparties on a single contract (ignoring fees and external hedging) is closest to:

explanations.A: Without external value creation, one side's P&L on the contract is the other's loss, so it is not positive-sum.
explanations.B: Pricing, sizing, and timing are all strategic decisions.
explanations.C: Their interests are opposed, not aligned, so it is not a coordination game.
explanations.D: Correct. The mark-to-market gain of one counterparty equals the loss of the other, making it a zero-sum transfer.

questions.question: Which of the following is always true of a strict Nash equilibrium?
options: each player's strategy is the unique best response to the others

explanations.A: Strict Nash equilibria are in pure strategies; mixed equilibria are generally not strict.
explanations.B: Payoffs can be negative; the equilibrium concept does not require positivity.
explanations.C: Correct. In a strict Nash equilibrium each player's action is the unique best response, so any deviation strictly lowers their payoff.
explanations.D: Equilibria need not maximize total welfare (prisoner's dilemma is a counterexample).

questions.question: In the 'guess 2/3 of the average' game (numbers 0-100), iterated elimination of dominated strategies drives rational play toward:

explanations.A: Correct. Each round of reasoning lowers the rational guess; iterating to the limit drives the equilibrium guess to 0.
explanations.B: 100 is dominated immediately since 2/3 of any average is at most ~67.
explanations.C: 50 is what naive (level-0) players might pick, but it is eliminated by further rounds of reasoning.
explanations.D: Rational iterated reasoning converges to 0, not to randomness.

questions.question: In the beauty-contest / 'guess 2/3 of the average' game, why might guessing exactly 0 be suboptimal against real human opponents?
options: real players use only a few levels of reasoning, so the average is usually above 0, making a small positive guess better

explanations.A: 0 is the Nash equilibrium, not a dominated strategy.
explanations.B: Correct. Empirically, people reason only a few steps (level-k), so the winning guess is typically well above 0 — best-responding to actual behavior beats assuming full rationality.
explanations.C: The Nash equilibrium is 0, not 100.
explanations.D: Guessing is the entire game; it is certainly allowed.

questions.question: Two gas stations across the street can set a High or Low price. Each is tempted to undercut, but mutual Low prices erode both their margins versus mutual High. Without collusion, the likely equilibrium is:

explanations.A: Correct. Undercutting is individually attractive regardless of the rival, so the prisoner's-dilemma logic pushes both to Low prices.
explanations.B: Both-High is unstable: each can grab share by undercutting to Low.
explanations.C: A permanent asymmetric split is not an equilibrium; the High-priced station would cut prices.
explanations.D: There is a stable equilibrium — mutual Low pricing.

questions.question: Why is tacit collusion (both keeping prices High) easier to sustain in a repeated game than in a one-shot game?
options: one-shot games have no Nash equilibrium
future punishment for undercutting can deter a present deviation if players are patient

explanations.A: Prices remain central; repetition changes incentives, not relevance.
explanations.B: Repetition does not turn a pricing game into a zero-sum game.
explanations.C: One-shot games do have equilibria (mutual undercutting here); that is the problem repetition helps overcome.
explanations.D: Correct. In repeated play the threat of losing future cooperative profits can outweigh the one-time gain from undercutting, sustaining high prices.

questions.question: An inspector audits a trader with probability p; the trader cheats with probability q. The trader is deterred (indifferent) when the audit probability makes expected cheating payoff equal to honest payoff. This illustrates that:
options: in the mixed equilibrium each side randomizes to keep the other indifferent

explanations.A: Always auditing is costly; the equilibrium uses a calibrated probability, not certainty.
explanations.B: Correct. Inspection games have mixed equilibria where the inspector audits just often enough to make cheating unprofitable and the trader cheats just enough to keep auditing worthwhile.
explanations.C: Always cheating is deterred precisely by the equilibrium audit probability.
explanations.D: Auditing with the right probability is exactly what deters cheating.

questions.question: In a second-price auction your private value is $50. Bidding $60 instead of $50:
options: can only hurt you: you might win and pay a price above $50
has no effect on outcomes ever

explanations.A: Overbidding cannot increase profit; it only risks winning at a loss.
explanations.B: The dominant strategy is to bid your true value $50, not $60.
explanations.C: Correct. Overbidding can win cases where the second price is between $50 and $60, forcing you to pay more than the item is worth to you.
explanations.D: It does change outcomes in the range where the second-highest bid falls between $50 and $60.

questions.question: Two bidders have values uniform on [0,1]. The symmetric first-price equilibrium bid is half of value, b(v) = v/2. A bidder with value 0.8 bids:

explanations.A: 0.8 is the true value; the equilibrium prescribes shading to half.
explanations.B: 0.2 would be v/4, more shading than the equilibrium rule.
explanations.C: Correct. With two bidders, b(v) = v/2, so a value of 0.8 maps to a bid of 0.4.
explanations.D: 0.6 is between value and v/2 but not the equilibrium bid v/2 = 0.4.

questions.question: The fundamental trade-off a first-price bidder faces when choosing how much to shade is:
options: lower bids raise surplus-if-win but lower the probability of winning
higher bids always raise total profit
shading has no effect on winning

explanations.A: Correct. Shading more increases your profit margin conditional on winning but reduces how often you win, and the optimum balances the two.
explanations.B: Higher bids reduce surplus-when-winning, so they do not always raise profit.
explanations.C: Shading directly lowers your win probability, so it certainly affects winning.
explanations.D: The win-probability vs surplus trade-off is exactly what defines optimal shading.

questions.question: In an ultimatum game, the proposer offers a split of $10 and the responder accepts or rejects (rejection gives both $0). The subgame-perfect prediction is that the proposer offers:
options: the smallest positive amount, expecting the responder to accept anything over $0

explanations.A: A 50/50 split is common in experiments due to fairness, but it is not the subgame-perfect prediction.
explanations.B: If the proposer offers nothing, a self-interested responder is indifferent/may reject; the SPE offer is the smallest positive amount to the responder.
explanations.C: Giving everything away is not payoff-maximizing for the proposer.
explanations.D: Correct. A rational responder accepts any positive amount over $0, so backward induction has the proposer offer the minimal positive share and keep the rest.

questions.question: Real human responders in the ultimatum game often reject low offers. This shows that:
options: backward induction with purely selfish, money-maximizing preferences does not fully predict behavior
the proposer has a dominant strategy to offer zero

explanations.A: The game does have a subgame-perfect equilibrium; the point is behavior deviates from it.
explanations.B: Correct. Rejections reveal preferences over fairness, so the purely-selfish backward-induction prediction misses real behavior.
explanations.C: Given fairness preferences, rejecting an insulting offer can be rational; it is not always irrational.
explanations.D: Offering zero is not even the standard SPE (which is a minimal positive amount), and humans punish low offers.

questions.question: A trade has a 60% chance of +$300 and a 40% chance of -$500. Its expected value is:

explanations.A: +$100 does not match the probability-weighted computation.
explanations.B: +$180 is only the favorable term, ignoring the 40% loss.
explanations.C: -$200 is only the loss term, ignoring the 60% gain.
explanations.D: Correct. EV = 0.6×300 + 0.4×(-500) = 180 - 200 = -$20, so the trade is slightly negative.

questions.question: For a positive-EV strategy with high variance, the main reason to size positions small (e.g. via the Kelly criterion) is to:
options: avoid risk of ruin and maximize long-run growth despite positive EV

explanations.A: Sizing does not lower per-unit EV; it manages risk while keeping the edge.
explanations.B: Position sizing has nothing to do with making a game zero-sum.
explanations.C: Correct. Even with positive EV, oversizing risks ruin from variance; controlled sizing protects capital and maximizes long-run geometric growth.
explanations.D: No sizing guarantees per-trade profit; variance still produces losing trades.

questions.question: Consider a 2x2 game. Row's payoffs: Up gives (4 vs L, 3 vs R); Down gives (2 vs L, 1 vs R). For Row, Up is:

explanations.A: Correct. Up beats Down whether Column plays L (4>2) or R (3>1), so Up strictly dominates Down.
explanations.B: Down is dominated, not dominant; Up beats it everywhere.
explanations.C: Up beats Down against both L and R, not only L.
explanations.D: Up beats Down against both columns, not only R.

questions.question: In a 2x2 game, suppose (Up, Left) gives Row its best payoff in the Left column AND gives Column its best payoff in the Up row. Then (Up, Left) is:
options: only an equilibrium if payoffs are equal

explanations.A: Mutual best responses always form an equilibrium, so 'never' is wrong.
explanations.B: Correct. Each player is best-responding (Row best in Left, Column best in Up), which is exactly the mutual best-response definition of Nash equilibrium.
explanations.C: Equal payoffs are not required for equilibrium.
explanations.D: A profile of mutual best responses is not dominated by definition.

questions.question: Why does a Nash equilibrium in mixed strategies still satisfy the 'no profitable deviation' rule even though a player is randomizing?
options: because every action in the mix yields equal expected payoff, so no pure or mixed deviation does better
because the opponent cannot observe the mix

explanations.A: Correct. At a mixed equilibrium the player is indifferent among the actions mixed over, so no deviation (pure or mixed) raises expected payoff.
explanations.B: Randomizing is not always best; it is only best when the mixed actions tie in expected payoff.
explanations.C: Equilibrium reasoning assumes the opponent knows your strategy; unobservability is not the reason.
explanations.D: Mixed strategies are chosen precisely to optimize expected payoff.

questions.question: In a zero-sum game, a strategy that maximizes your minimum guaranteed payoff is called your:

explanations.A: Tit-for-tat is a repeated-game cooperation rule, unrelated to single-shot maximin.
explanations.B: A dominated strategy is one to avoid, not a security strategy.
explanations.C: Zero-sum games have no cooperative gains, so a cooperative strategy is not meaningful here.
explanations.D: Correct. A maximin strategy maximizes the worst-case (minimum) payoff, and in zero-sum games it coincides with the equilibrium/minimax strategy.

questions.question: In matching pennies, suppose Row deviates to playing heads 70% of the time while Column still mixes 50/50. Row's expected payoff:

explanations.A: Against a 50/50 opponent every Row mix yields the same expected payoff, so it cannot rise above zero.
explanations.B: Correct. When Column mixes 50/50, Row is indifferent among all mixes, so any deviation still yields expected payoff zero — illustrating the indifference condition.
explanations.C: It does not fall either; the payoff is flat at zero against an equal mixer.
explanations.D: The expected payoff is well-defined and equals zero.

questions.question: In a 2x2 game, a player's equilibrium mixing probability is determined by:
options: the opponent's payoffs (via making the opponent indifferent)

explanations.A: Counterintuitively, your own payoffs do not set your own mix; they set the opponent's mix.
explanations.B: There is no referee in the model; the mix follows from the indifference conditions.
explanations.C: Correct. Your mix is chosen to make the opponent indifferent, so it is pinned down by the opponent's payoffs.
explanations.D: In a one-shot 2x2 game the equilibrium mix does not depend on a round count.

questions.question: Which payoff ranking (for each player) defines a prisoner's dilemma, where T=temptation, R=reward, P=punishment, S=sucker?

explanations.A: This ordering makes cooperation dominant, not a dilemma.
explanations.B: If R > T, cooperation would be a best response and the dilemma disappears.
explanations.C: Correct. T > R > P > S makes defecting dominant (T beats R, P beats S) while mutual cooperation R beats mutual defection P — the defining ordering.
explanations.D: P > R would make mutual defection jointly preferred, removing the dilemma's tension.

questions.question: An option-like bet pays $0 with probability 0.9 and $100 with probability 0.1. A fair price (zero expected profit) for entering it is:

explanations.A: Correct. Expected payout = 0.1×$100 = $10, so paying $10 makes the bet break even.
explanations.B: $100 is the max payout, far above the $10 expected value.
explanations.C: $0 would give the buyer a free positive-EV bet, not a fair price.
explanations.D: $50 overpays relative to the $10 expected payout.

questions.question: If a strategy is strictly dominated, then in any Nash equilibrium it is played with probability:

explanations.A: A strictly dominated strategy is never a best response, so it cannot be played with certainty in equilibrium.
explanations.B: Regardless of the opponent, a strictly dominated strategy is never optimal, so the weight is always zero.
explanations.C: It gets zero weight, not one-half.
explanations.D: Correct. A strictly dominated strategy is never a best response, so it receives zero probability in any Nash equilibrium.

questions.question: In the centipede game, backward induction predicts that the first player will:
options: continue cooperating until the very end
take/stop at the very first opportunity

explanations.A: Backward induction unravels cooperation: anticipating the last-mover taking, each earlier player takes too.
explanations.B: Correct. Folding back from the final node, each player prefers to take immediately, so the first player stops at the first chance.
explanations.C: The backward-induction solution is a pure 'take' action, not randomization.
explanations.D: Always passing is not subgame perfect; the player anticipates being exploited at the end.

questions.question: A trader claims 'we're at equilibrium because everyone is making money.' The flaw is that Nash equilibrium is about:
options: no player having a profitable unilateral deviation, regardless of profit levels

explanations.A: Profitability does not determine equilibrium; a profitable situation can still have beneficial deviations.
explanations.B: Maximizing total profit is a cooperative goal, distinct from Nash equilibrium.
explanations.C: Equal profits are not required for equilibrium.
explanations.D: Correct. Equilibrium is defined by the absence of any profitable unilateral deviation, not by how much anyone earns.

questions.question: The support of a mixed strategy is:
options: the total payoff of the game
the player with the higher payoff
the set of actions assigned positive probability

explanations.A: It is a set of actions, not a payoff total.
explanations.B: Support refers to actions, not to which player earns more.
explanations.C: Correct. The support is exactly the set of actions the player plays with strictly positive probability.
explanations.D: It has nothing to do with the number of rounds.

questions.question: In equilibrium, a player assigns positive probability to two actions. This requires that, against the opponent's strategy, those two actions have:
options: the same expected payoff, which is also at least as high as any unused action

explanations.A: Correct. Actions in the support must tie in expected payoff, and that common value must weakly exceed any action left out of the support.
explanations.B: Different expected payoffs would make the player drop the worse action, contradicting mixing.
explanations.C: The equal expected payoff need not be zero.
explanations.D: Variance does not govern which actions enter the support; expected payoff does.

questions.question: Which transformation turns a constant-sum game (payoffs always sum to c) into an equivalent zero-sum game?
options: subtracting c/2 from each player's payoffs

explanations.A: Doubling keeps the sum at 2c, not zero.
explanations.B: Correct. Subtracting c/2 from each player's payoff makes the totals sum to zero while preserving all strategic incentives.
explanations.C: Adding c raises the sum further, away from zero.
explanations.D: Constant-sum games are strategically equivalent to zero-sum games via a constant shift, so it can be done.

questions.question: A market maker quoting a two-sided market 'shades' their bid below fair value primarily to:
options: build in a margin against adverse selection and earn the spread

explanations.A: Correct. Bidding below fair value (and offering above) earns the spread and cushions against informed flow — the market-making analogue of bid shading.
explanations.B: Quoting below fair value still attracts sellers; the goal is not to avoid trading.
explanations.C: Shading is designed to protect profit, not to lose money.
explanations.D: Quoting choices do not change whether the underlying game is zero-sum.

questions.question: A key practical reason auction designers like the second-price (Vickrey) format is that it:
options: eliminates the need for any bids
always raises more revenue than any other format
induces truthful bidding, simplifying strategy for participants

explanations.A: Second-price auctions remove the incentive to shade; truthful bidding is dominant.
explanations.B: Bids are still required; they determine the winner and the price.
explanations.C: By revenue equivalence it does not systematically beat other standard formats.
explanations.D: Correct. Because payment depends on others' bids, truthful bidding is dominant, so participants don't need complex strategic shading.

questions.question: Two strategies: X has EV +$2 with std dev $1; Y has EV +$2 with std dev $20. For a risk-neutral trader maximizing expected P&L only, they are:

explanations.A: Lower variance appeals to a risk-averse trader, but a purely risk-neutral one ranks by EV, which ties.
explanations.B: Correct. Both have EV +$2, so a risk-neutral trader (who ignores variance) is indifferent between them.
explanations.C: Higher variance does not help a risk-neutral trader; the EVs are equal.
explanations.D: Both have positive EV (+$2), not negative.

questions.question: A 'dominant strategy equilibrium' is especially robust because it:
options: requires players to correctly predict opponents' strategies
does not depend on beliefs about what opponents will do

explanations.A: Its strength is precisely that it does NOT require predicting opponents.
explanations.B: Dominant-strategy equilibria appear in many non-zero-sum games (e.g. prisoner's dilemma).
explanations.C: Correct. Each player's dominant strategy is optimal regardless of others' choices, so the equilibrium holds without any beliefs about opponents.
explanations.D: Dominant strategies are pure; no randomization is needed.

questions.question: Which statement about Nash equilibrium and dominant strategies is correct?
options: every Nash equilibrium uses dominant strategies
dominant strategies and Nash equilibria are the same concept
if every player has a dominant strategy, the dominant-strategy profile is a Nash equilibrium, but Nash equilibria can exist without dominant strategies
Nash equilibria never involve dominant strategies

explanations.A: Many Nash equilibria (e.g. matching pennies' mixed equilibrium) involve no dominant strategies.
explanations.B: They are related but distinct: dominance is a stronger, action-level property.
explanations.C: Correct. Dominant strategies imply a Nash equilibrium, but the converse fails — most games have Nash equilibria without any dominant strategy.
explanations.D: When dominant strategies exist they do form a Nash equilibrium, so 'never' is false.

questions.question: In the goalie-vs-kicker game, the kicker scores 90% kicking to their strong side if the goalie guesses wrong but only 60% to their weak side if wrong. A correct implication for equilibrium play is that the kicker:
options: should mix, but the exact frequencies depend on both sides' success probabilities, not just the strong side
should always kick to the strong side
should always kick to the weak side

explanations.A: Correct. The equilibrium requires mixing, and the optimal frequencies are pinned down by the full payoff matrix (both sides' success rates), keeping the goalie indifferent.
explanations.B: Always kicking strong is predictable and exploitable; the goalie would always guess that side.
explanations.C: Always kicking weak is also predictable and suboptimal.
explanations.D: The kicker is not indifferent across all strategies; the equilibrium mix is specific to the probabilities.

questions.question: Why is poker (heads-up, fixed stakes, ignoring the rake) modeled as zero-sum but the broader trading ecosystem (with real investment) often positive-sum?
options: poker chips are transferred between players, while productive investment can create new value for everyone

explanations.A: The broader trading ecosystem is not always zero-sum, which is exactly the contrast.
explanations.B: Poker is intensely strategic; that is not the distinction.
explanations.C: Trading certainly has losers; the point is total value can still rise.
explanations.D: Correct. In poker one player's winnings are another's losses (a pure transfer), whereas real investment can grow the total pie, making the broader system positive-sum.

questions.question: In a prisoner's dilemma, communication before a one-shot play (without enforcement) typically:
options: does not change the equilibrium, since promises to cooperate are not binding and defection remains dominant

explanations.A: Cheap talk cannot guarantee cooperation when defection is still individually optimal.
explanations.B: Correct. Without enforcement, defection remains the dominant strategy, so non-binding pre-play talk leaves the equilibrium (mutual defection) unchanged.
explanations.C: Talking does not alter payoffs, so cooperation does not become dominant.
explanations.D: Communication does not change the payoff structure to zero-sum.

questions.question: Two liquidity providers choose to post on Venue A or Venue B. Each earns more on the venue the other avoids (less competition). A profile where both post on the same venue is:
options: not a Nash equilibrium, since each would prefer to switch to the emptier venue

explanations.A: If both crowd one venue, each can profit by moving to the emptier one, so it fails the no-deviation test.
explanations.B: This is a standard congestion-style game and is straightforward to analyze.
explanations.C: Crowding the same venue lowers each one's profit, so it is not optimal.
explanations.D: Correct. Because each provider does better where the other isn't, both-on-same-venue invites a profitable deviation and is not an equilibrium; the equilibria have them split.

questions.question: Three identical liquidity providers can each enter a thin market that profitably supports only one. A symmetric equilibrium typically has each provider:
options: entering with a probability less than 1 (a mixed entry strategy)

explanations.A: If all always enter, the market is overcrowded and each loses, so always-enter is not an equilibrium.
explanations.B: If all stay out, any one could profitably enter, so never-enter is not an equilibrium either.
explanations.C: Correct. Symmetric entry games have a mixed equilibrium where each enters with a probability that makes players indifferent between entering and staying out.
explanations.D: There is no coordinator; the symmetric solution is a mixed strategy.

questions.question: A signal predicts a stock will rise with probability 0.55 (gain $1) and fall with probability 0.45 (lose $1). Expected value per trade is:

explanations.B: +$0.55 is just the win probability times the gain, ignoring the losing side.
explanations.C: -$0.10 reverses the sign; the edge is positive since wins are more likely.
explanations.D: $0 would require equal win/loss probabilities, but 0.55 ≠ 0.45.

questions.question: A lottery ticket costs $2 and pays $1,000,000 with probability 1 in 10,000,000. The expected value of buying one ticket (payout minus cost) is approximately:

explanations.A: The expected payout is only $0.10, but you must subtract the $2 cost, giving a negative net.
explanations.B: Correct. Expected payout = 1,000,000/10,000,000 = $0.10; minus the $2 cost gives -$1.90.
explanations.C: +$98 has no basis; the win is extraordinarily unlikely.
explanations.D: Break-even would need an expected payout of $2, but it is only $0.10.

questions.question: In an entry-deterrence game, an incumbent's threat to flood the market and trigger losses for both is credible only if:
options: the incumbent has pre-committed capacity that makes fighting its actual best response
the threat is never credible under any condition

explanations.A: Correct. A sunk commitment (e.g. installed capacity) can make fighting profitable after entry, turning an otherwise empty threat into a credible, subgame-perfect one.
explanations.B: Mere announcements are cheap talk and do not establish credibility.
explanations.C: The entrant's risk attitude does not make the incumbent's threat credible.
explanations.D: Threats can be made credible via commitment, so 'never' is too strong.

questions.question: In a first-price auction, holding your value fixed, lowering your bid:
options: increases the price you pay if you win
has no effect on your surplus
increases your surplus if you win but reduces your probability of winning

explanations.A: Lowering your bid lowers, not raises, what you pay when you win.
explanations.B: A lower bid makes winning less likely, certainly not guaranteed.
explanations.C: It directly changes surplus-when-winning, so it does have an effect.
explanations.D: Correct. A lower bid means more surplus (value minus bid) when you win, but you win less often — the central first-price trade-off.

questions.question: In a second-price auction with values $30, $45, and $70 (all bidding truthfully), the winner and price are:

explanations.A: Paying your own bid is the first-price rule, not second-price.
explanations.B: Correct. The highest bidder ($70) wins and pays the second-highest bid, $45.
explanations.C: The $45 bidder does not win; the $70 bidder is highest.
explanations.D: The price is the second-highest bid ($45), not the third ($30).

questions.question: All-pay auctions (every bidder pays their bid whether or not they win) model contests like lobbying. Compared to a standard auction, bidders here tend to:
options: shade bids to account for paying even when they lose

explanations.A: Since you pay regardless, the cost of losing is central and cannot be ignored.
explanations.B: Bidding full value ignores the risk of paying that amount while losing.
explanations.C: Correct. Because losers also pay, rational bidders shade carefully to manage the expected cost of bidding without winning.
explanations.D: Participation can still be worthwhile; the prize draws bids despite the all-pay rule.

questions.question: Mechanism design is sometimes called 'reverse game theory' because it:
options: predicts behavior in a fixed game
designs the rules so that self-interested play leads to a desired outcome

explanations.A: Predicting behavior in a given game is ordinary game theory; mechanism design goes the other way.
explanations.B: It works with strategic behavior, channeling it, rather than removing strategy.
explanations.C: Correct. You start from a desired outcome and engineer the rules/incentives so rational, self-interested players produce it.
explanations.D: Mechanism design applies broadly (auctions, voting, matching), not just zero-sum games.

questions.question: A mechanism is 'incentive compatible' (truthful) when:
options: each participant's best strategy is to report their true preferences/values
the designer must monitor every player
all players are forced to randomize

explanations.A: Correct. In an incentive-compatible mechanism, honest reporting is a best response (e.g. truthful bidding in a Vickrey auction).
explanations.B: Incentive compatibility means the opposite — truth-telling, not lying, is optimal.
explanations.C: The point is to avoid needing monitoring by aligning incentives with truth-telling.
explanations.D: Truthful reporting is typically a pure strategy, not forced randomization.

questions.question: You may pay $1 to flip a fair coin: heads returns $3 (net +$2), tails returns $0 (net -$1). Should a risk-neutral player play?

explanations.A: Whether the game is zero-sum is irrelevant to a single player's positive EV decision.
explanations.B: The sign is wrong; the expected net value is positive.
explanations.C: It is not break-even; the favorable payoff makes EV positive.
explanations.D: Correct. EV = 0.5×(+2) + 0.5×(-1) = 1 - 0.5 = +$0.50, so a risk-neutral player should play.

questions.question: In the Cournot duopoly, each firm chooses a quantity; the Nash equilibrium is the pair of quantities where:
options: the firms jointly maximize total profit
each firm's quantity is a best response to the other's quantity

explanations.A: Joint profit maximization is the collusive (monopoly) outcome, which differs from and exceeds Cournot output.
explanations.B: Correct. The Cournot equilibrium is where each firm's output maximizes its profit given the other's output — mutual best response.
explanations.C: A single firm producing everything is the monopoly case, not the duopoly Nash equilibrium.
explanations.D: Producing zero is not a best response when positive output is profitable.

questions.question: Relative to the Cournot (competitive) equilibrium, the collusive cartel outcome features:
options: higher total output and lower prices
lower total output and higher prices, but each firm is tempted to cheat by overproducing

explanations.A: Collusion restricts output to raise prices, the opposite of higher output and lower prices.
explanations.B: Collusion raises profits relative to competition; it does not eliminate them.
explanations.C: Collusive output is strictly below the Cournot level, so they are not identical.
explanations.D: Correct. Cartels cut output to lift prices and profits, but each member has a private incentive to overproduce — the instability at the heart of collusion.

questions.question: An options market maker who always widens quotes right before economic releases becomes predictable. A strategic counterparty could:
options: do nothing useful with that information
be forced to pay wider spreads forever
time orders around the predictable pattern to trade at better prices against the maker

explanations.A: Predictability is exploitable information, so 'nothing useful' is wrong.
explanations.B: The counterparty can route around the predictable widening, so it is not forced to pay wider spreads.
explanations.C: Correct. Knowing exactly when the maker widens lets a counterparty time trades to avoid the wide quotes or pick off stale ones — exactly why randomizing/varying behavior helps.
explanations.D: Exploiting predictability is adversarial, not cooperative.

questions.question: Two algorithmic strategies trade exclusively against each other in a closed simulation with no external flow. Over time their combined P&L (ignoring costs) must be:

explanations.A: Correct. It is a closed zero-sum system: every dollar one strategy makes is lost by the other, so combined P&L is zero.
explanations.B: With no external value source, gains for one are losses for the other, so the total cannot be positive.
explanations.C: Combined P&L is exactly zero, not negative (there are no external costs in this setup).
explanations.D: The total is fixed at zero; only the split changes, so it cannot grow.

questions.question: A trade offers +$10 with probability 0.3, +$2 with probability 0.5, and -$20 with probability 0.2. Its expected value is:

explanations.A: +$4 omits the -$20 loss contribution.
explanations.B: Correct. EV = 0.3×10 + 0.5×2 + 0.2×(-20) = 3 + 1 - 4 = $0, exactly break-even.
explanations.C: -$4 counts only the loss term and drops the two positive terms.
explanations.D: +$8 overstates the positive contributions and ignores the loss.

questions.question: In a sealed-bid second-price auction, why is overbidding (above value) weakly dominated?
options: it never helps and sometimes forces you to win at a price above your value
it always lowers the price you pay
it guarantees a loss every time
it is actually the dominant strategy

explanations.A: Correct. Overbidding only changes outcomes by making you win when the second price exceeds your value — a money-losing win — and never helps, so it is weakly dominated.
explanations.B: Your bid does not set the price you pay (the second-highest bid does), so overbidding does not lower your price.
explanations.C: It does not lose every time; it only hurts in the range where the second price lies between your value and your bid.
explanations.D: The dominant strategy is truthful bidding, not overbidding.

questions.question: Backward induction requires which informational assumption to apply cleanly?
options: perfect information (players observe all prior moves)

explanations.A: It applies most cleanly to finite-horizon games; an infinite horizon complicates the last-node logic.
explanations.B: Simultaneous moves create imperfect information, where simple backward induction over a tree does not directly apply.
explanations.C: Backward induction works regardless of whether payoffs are zero-sum.
explanations.D: Correct. Standard backward induction applies to finite games of perfect information, where each player sees the history before moving.

questions.question: In matching pennies, if Column is known to play heads 60% of the time, Row (who wants to match) should best-respond by playing:

explanations.A: Always tails minimizes matches against a heads-leaning Column, the worst choice for Row.
explanations.B: Correct. Since Row wins by matching and Column favors heads, Row's best response is to always play heads to maximize matches.
explanations.C: Mixing is only optimal when indifferent; against a 60% heads opponent Row strictly prefers heads.
explanations.D: Copying the opponent's frequency is not a best response; pure heads scores more matches here.

questions.question: If, against the opponent's equilibrium mix, your action A pays 5 and action B pays 7, then in equilibrium you must be:
options: mixing equally over A and B
playing only B, and the opponent's mix must actually make A and B equal if you are to mix

explanations.A: You would never mix when B strictly pays more; equal mixing is not a best response here.
explanations.B: You would not play the worse action A when B pays more.
explanations.C: Correct. With B strictly better you play only B; for you to genuinely mix in equilibrium the opponent's strategy must equalize A and B, which it doesn't here.
explanations.D: You are not indifferent — B strictly beats A — so the indifference condition is violated.

questions.question: A bet wins $b with probability p and loses $1 with probability (1-p). It is break-even (zero EV) when:

explanations.A: b = 1 - p ignores the win probability multiplying b.
explanations.B: p = b confuses a probability with a payoff and is not the zero-EV condition.
explanations.C: Correct. EV = p·b - (1-p)·1; setting it to zero gives p·b = (1 - p), the break-even condition.
explanations.D: p·b = p would force b = 1 regardless of p, which is not the general break-even rule.

questions.question: Using break-even logic, if a trade risks $1 to make $3, the minimum win probability needed for non-negative EV is:

explanations.A: Correct. Break-even: p×3 = (1-p)×1 → 3p = 1 - p → 4p = 1 → p = 25%.
explanations.B: 75% is far above the threshold; the favorable 3:1 payoff means you need far fewer wins.
explanations.C: 50% would be the threshold only for a 1:1 payoff, not 3:1.
explanations.D: 33% corresponds to a 2:1 payoff, not 3:1.

questions.question: Which is the most precise statement of why the prisoner's dilemma's (Defect, Defect) is a Nash equilibrium?
options: because both players earn their highest possible payoff
because given the other player defects, each player's best response is also to defect

explanations.A: (Defect, Defect) does not maximize joint payoff — mutual cooperation does.
explanations.B: Communication is irrelevant; the equilibrium holds from incentives alone.
explanations.C: Both players earn less than under mutual cooperation, so it is not their highest payoff.
explanations.D: Correct. Each player defecting is a best response to the other's defection, satisfying the mutual best-response definition of Nash equilibrium.

questions.question: A defender protects one of two assets; an attacker strikes one. The defender values asset 1 more. In the mixed equilibrium, the defender should:
options: defend the higher-value asset more often, but still randomize to avoid predictability

explanations.A: Always defending asset 1 lets the attacker safely hit asset 2; pure defense is exploitable.
explanations.B: Correct. The equilibrium tilts protection toward the more valuable asset while still randomizing so the attacker can't exploit a fixed pattern.
explanations.C: Always defending the lower-value asset leaves the prized asset exposed.
explanations.D: Asset values shape the optimal mix, so they cannot be ignored.
